The participant recovered by re-examining the structure and accepted the dual-purpose design, but the initial comprehension friction required navigation backtracking and verbal clarification.

finding: Participant experienced uncertainty about page structure and content scope, requiring clarification that the page contained two separate topics rather than one unified topic.
observed_signal: Participant navigated through headings using keyboard navigation (pressing H) and read through the page content, then reflected on initial confusion about the page's organization.
stated_signal: when I actually started reading then I realized it was two separate things, but just if if I knew that beforehand, then I could have prepared myself that that as well I was actually going to be accessing here... it was a bit clearer about what kind of help I'm going to get on this page. I think that would, that would be beneficial.

The heading hierarchy did not adequately communicate upfront that there were two distinct topic areas (website accessibility and accessing services). This caused brief confusion that was resolved through reading, but the participant noted it would have been helpful to know beforehand. This represents mild but recoverable comprehension friction.
structural_amplification_note: Keyboard navigation user (screen reader implied) relied on heading hierarchy for wayfinding and page structure understanding; unclear heading organization amplified comprehension difficulty.

finding: Participant struggled to navigate the table columns using keyboard shortcuts and screen reader commands, requiring repeated attempts to read column headers.
observed_signal: Participant pressed T to locate the table, identified it as having 3 columns and 29 rows, then attempted to use control+alt with arrow keys to read column headers, but the transcript shows incomplete or unclear header information ('Comprehensively more about comprehensive column 2 of 3').
stated_signal: I'm going to press T to go to the table. I'm going to read the columns first of all, so I'm going to press control alt and then just go along just to read the column headers.
rationale: The participant is using keyboard and assistive technology navigation to access table content, but the column header output is garbled or incomplete ('Comprehensively more about comprehensive column 2 of 3'), indicating the screen reader is not properly announcing the column structure. This represents multiple attempts at a standard AT pattern without clear success, matching accessibility friction with significant struggle.
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader user relying on keyboard navigation and AT-specific commands (control+alt arrows) to access table structure; incomplete or malformed header announcement amplifies barrier for blind or low-vision users.

finding: Participant encountered confusing table layout with blank cells that obscured heading structure and made navigation disorienting with a screen reader.
observed_signal: Participant navigated through table cells using arrow keys, repeatedly encountered blank cells, cycled through redundant cell readings (e.g., 'comprehensive, more about comprehensive'), and expressed confusion about which cells were headings versus content.
stated_signal: Yeah, see that can get a bit confusing. Having blank cells in tables that can just confuse things. If I just went to availability first of all, and I just went along, then I wouldn't have known that that is just a heading, so it's not even said that it's a heading.
rationale: The participant using screen reader navigation (indicated by keyboard cell traversal and cell-by-cell reading) encountered a table with blank cells and unmarked heading rows that caused repeated confusion about structure. Both observed cycling behaviour and stated frustration confirm that the table accessibility semantics (missing heading markup, blank cells) created significant friction. This matches S4 (multiple failed attempts, detours, extra cognitive effort) and L3 (struggle with multiple attempts).
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader or keyboard user attempting to navigate a table with poor heading markup and blank cells experiences structurally higher friction than a sighted user, as the visual hierarchy is lost in non-visual navigation.

finding: Participant encountered a table with multiple blank cells and could not determine whether the blanks represented unavailable offerings or missing data, creating confusion about content availability.
observed_signal: Participant navigated through a table using keyboard commands (arrow keys, column/row navigation), repeatedly encountering blank cells across multiple rows and columns, and explicitly inspected rows 2–20 to check for information.
stated_signal: I don't know if these are meant to be blank or as blank as in this isn't on offer. These are blanks. All the cells are blank, so it's not really giving me any information at all.
rationale: The participant could not comprehend the meaning of the blank table cells and stated this ambiguity explicitly. The repeated navigation attempts to verify whether data existed elsewhere, combined with the stated conclusion that no information was provided, demonstrate significant friction. The participant's observation and frustrated tone indicate they struggled to understand the table structure and content, matching the L3 gate (significant friction with frustration and multiple attempts to understand).
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader or keyboard-reliant users would experience amplified friction due to the lack of table headers, row/column labels, or explanatory text for blank cells, making it even harder to determine whether blanks signify missing data or intentional structure.

finding: Participant encountered confusion and uncertainty when attempting to activate links embedded within table cells, pressing Enter multiple times and expressing uncertainty about where navigation led.
observed_signal: Participant pressed Enter to activate a link in a table cell, navigated back, re-entered the table, and attempted to activate the link again while expressing disorientation about the outcome.
stated_signal: I'm not sure where that's taking me. I'm not sure what happened there. So it's um always best if you can avoid it, not to have links that are in table cells if they've got other information there as well.
rationale: The participant explicitly stated uncertainty about link activation behaviour ('I'm not sure where that's taking me', 'I'm not sure what happened there') and demonstrated multiple attempts to understand and recover from the interaction. The repeated back-and-forth navigation and stated confusion indicate significant friction requiring multiple attempts before understanding the pattern.
structural_amplification_note: This friction is structurally amplified for keyboard and assistive-technology users, who rely on predictable table cell navigation and clear link activation semantics. Embedded links within table cells create ambiguity about whether Enter activates the link or navigates the cell, directly impacting AT user confidence.

finding: Participant identified a structural accessibility issue with blank cells in table headers, noting this as a design best practice violation that creates potential navigation confusion.
observed_signal: Participant verbally identified and flagged a blank cell in the first column of a table (column 103) during review.
stated_signal: Blank column 103. I've got a blank cell there, so tables, it's best if you don't have a blank cell, if you can avoid it. I know it's the first cell, um in the table, so it's um, uh, potentially it could be blank because the information is going in the rows and columns, um, but if you could have something in there that just, um, it's just best not to have blank uh cells in a table.
rationale: The participant identified a blank table cell as an accessibility concern and explained the best practice rationale in matter-of-fact tones. While the issue was noted and explained rather than experienced as a blocker during task execution, it represents a recognized accessibility friction point that could impact AT users navigating tables.
structural_amplification_note: Blank table header cells create significant friction for screen reader users, who encounter empty labels and may lose contextual information during cell navigation. This structural issue directly impairs accessible table comprehension for users relying on AT.

finding: Screen reader user unable to access table content showing plan coverage (ticks, crosses, yes/no indicators) because assistive technology did not pick up the information, rendering the comparison table unusable for decision-making.
observed_signal: Participant scrolled through the table, heard column headers and labels read aloud, but confirmed no data values (ticks, crosses, yes/no) were being conveyed by the screen reader. Participant stated the table provides no information and is not helpful for making decisions about plan inclusions.
stated_signal: This table doesn't give me any information at all... My screen reader doesn't seem to be picking any of that up... there's no nothing that my screen reader can access there. So yeah, that table isn't really helpful for me to make any kind of decision or know what each plan includes.
rationale: The participant, a screen reader user, cannot access critical content (plan coverage indicators) within a comparison table that is core to the task outcome. Both observed behaviour (scrolling, hearing blank cells) and stated signal (explicit confirmation that the table is inaccessible and unhelpful) demonstrate accessibility friction. The component task—determining which plan includes a benefit—is blocked because the data is not conveyed to assistive technology, meeting the S2 gate (component task completely blocked).
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader user cohort experiences structural amplification: table markup lacks semantic indicators (checkmarks, yes/no values) in a form that assistive technology can parse, making the entire comparison matrix inaccessible. Sighted users likely see visual indicators that are stripped or not exposed to the accessibility tree.

finding: Participant encountered a link (compare assessments) that conflicted with task instructions not to navigate to another page, creating uncertainty about whether to proceed.
observed_signal: Participant noticed the compare assessments link, verbally acknowledged the constraint ('the instructions specifically said don't go to another page'), and decided to keep going down the page instead.
stated_signal: Well, there's a link compare assessments, but the the the instructions specifically said don't go to another page. Um, OK, so maybe, maybe leave that, keep going down
rationale: The participant understood the instruction constraint and the presence of the link but expressed brief uncertainty about the correct action ('maybe leave that'). The hesitation was minimal and quickly resolved through self-direction. This is one wayfinding uncertainty moment that did not block progress.

finding: Participant noted that the current table with ticks and crosses provided clearer information than a previous table, which lacked this visual distinction.
observed_signal: Participant compared the current assessment table (with green ticks and red crosses) to a previous table and explicitly stated the current one is 'more promising' and clearer.
stated_signal: Ah, OK, so this, this is a bit more promising. So again, in the previous table didn't have ticks or crosses, um, but here it seems to say.
rationale: The participant identified a prior comprehension barrier (lack of visual indicators in the previous table) and noted relief upon finding the current representation clearer. The mild positive valence ('more promising') and quick recovery indicate this was low friction with no task impediment.

finding: Participant expressed confusion about the relationship between different content sections showing overlapping information (price/duration vs. physical measurements with checkmarks).
observed_signal: Participant paused scrolling and asked clarifying questions about whether sections were related or separate, then continued scrolling without resolution.
stated_signal: I'm kind of confused now because in the top bit it just said 45 minutes and the price and now it's got green ticks. So is this the same section? Is this a different set? This is the physical measurements. So what does that relate to? Um, I'm, I'm not quite sure here.
rationale: The participant explicitly stated confusion about the structural relationship and meaning of repeated information presented in different formats. The stated confusion and observed hesitation align. The participant recovered by continuing to scroll, indicating brief but noticeable comprehension friction that did not block task completion.

finding: Participant expected to find a table on the page but the screen reader reported no tables present, creating uncertainty about whether the content exists or is marked up incorrectly.
observed_signal: Participant pressed T to navigate to tables, received 'There are no tables on this page' response twice, then expressed confusion that the page should contain table information despite the absence of detected tables.
stated_signal: There are no tables on this page. OK, see there's no tables on this page, but this is specifically about looking at the information in a table.
rationale: The participant is using a screen reader (pressing T for table navigation, H for headings) and encountered a mismatch: the task requires finding table information, but no tables are detected by assistive technology. This suggests either missing semantic markup or a content structure issue that blocks AT navigation. The participant expressed clear confusion and the component task (finding table information) cannot proceed via standard AT pathways.
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader user; the absence of proper table markup or semantic structure prevents the standard keyboard/AT navigation pattern (T key) from functioning, creating a structural barrier specific to assistive-technology reliance.

finding: Participant had to resort to heading navigation (H key) as a workaround after table navigation failed, indicating a non-optimal AT discovery pathway.
observed_signal: After T key yielded no tables, participant shifted to pressing H to navigate by headings and eventually located 'Compare health assessments' via heading structure.
stated_signal: OK, so let me just go up to the top. So I'm going to press H to maybe go down the page.
rationale: The participant recovered by using an alternative AT navigation method (headings) after the primary method (table navigation) failed. This is a noticeable friction event—requiring a detour—but the participant did locate the desired content ('Compare health assessments') and the task outcome was not at risk. Fits S5 and L2 (non-optimal path, recoverable, task completed).
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader user; reliance on semantic structure means that missing or poorly marked table elements force users to fall back to heading-based navigation, requiring extra cognitive effort to find content that sighted users would see directly.

finding: Participant expressed confusion about the layout and structure of the health assessment display, uncertain whether it was a table or other format, making it difficult to understand the content organization.
observed_signal: Participant navigated through the content, then stated confusion about the layout and said 'I wasn't really clear that that's what I was looking at' and noted uncertainty about whether it was a table format.
stated_signal: that was a bit confusing there. Um, I wasn't really clear that that's what I was looking at. If it was in a table, then that might be a bit clearer, but I don't think this is a table, or if it is, the screen just not picking up as a table and I can't navigate it as a table.
rationale: The participant expressed explicit confusion about understanding the layout and structure of the presented data. The stated confusion aligns with the observed hesitation and need to process the content after initially being unclear. The participant recovered after gaining clarity ('Now that I kind of know the way it's set out, it makes a bit more sense'), indicating noticeable but recoverable friction without blocking the task outcome.
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader or keyboard user would experience structural amplification if the content is not marked up as a proper table, as the participant noted the screen is not picking it up as a table and accessibility navigation is impaired.

finding: Participant could not navigate a comparison interface using arrow keys and encountered unexpected button behaviour that prevented interaction with page content.
observed_signal: Participant pressed up/down and left/right arrow keys with no response. Participant pressed H for headings, F for form field menu, and encountered a button that did not enable expected navigation. The participant completed the task eventually but with substantial detour and effort.

finding: Participant noted that Bupa's link text for phone number lacked context about what information the phone number was for, whereas Aviva's design with descriptive link text worked better.

finding: Participant observed that Bupa did not provide information about how long the company has been in business, whereas Aviva prominently displayed its 325-year history.

finding: Participant used keyboard navigation to search for headings on the AXA website but found none, blocking efficient navigation.

finding: Participant was uncertain about AXA's product range and scope based on the page title alone, contrasting negatively with a competitor's clearer information hierarchy.

finding: Participant used the keyboard shortcut F to navigate form fields but encountered a search button instead of the expected accordion form control for health assessment.

finding: Participant navigating with screen reader encountered non-descriptive button labels ('More information' and 'Select') that were not distinguishable from each other, forcing manual navigation to determine purpose.

The observed signal shows the participant had to abandon the form field navigation method and manually explore the page to understand button purpose. structural_amplification_note: Screen reader users are structurally dependent on descriptive labels to navigate controls. Generic labels like 'More information' and 'Select' without context force manual exploration, eliminating the efficiency of keyboard navigation and violating WCAG labeling requirements.

structural_amplification_note: If this participant is using assistive technology, random characters and numbers in form labels would be read aloud verbatim, amplifying comprehension friction and creating auditory noise that obscures the actual field purpose.

finding: Participant encountered unclear information architecture and could not determine which subscription type applied to their task, leading to navigation back to the top.

rationale: The participant expressed uncertainty about what the information meant and why it was presented. The observed behaviour shows disorientation and backtracking to restart navigation, indicating the information hierarchy or labeling did not clearly convey the purpose or relevance of each option. finding: Participant encountered extra, unclear information appearing alongside form field labels and controls, causing confusion about what data was required or expected.

This caused mild confusion about comprehensibility; the participant recovered by filtering the relevant information and proceeding. The friction is noticeable but did not block task completion.
structural_amplification_note: This issue is likely amplified for assistive-technology users, who must listen to all announced text without visual filtering and cannot quickly scan past irrelevant information.

finding: Participant encountered an undefined label when navigating form headings and expressed confusion about the lack of descriptive information.

finding: Participant had to parse through extraneous characters and non-essential content when reviewing form field options, requiring filtering to extract meaningful information.

finding: Participant encountered a button with a non-descriptive label (single quotation mark) that did not convey its purpose.

finding: Participant was confused by the meaning of the numeric indicators next to search distance options and could not confidently interpret what the numbers represented.

finding: Participant noted the absence of audio feedback when interacting with form controls, expressing concern about accessibility.

finding: Participant received no audio feedback when the page refreshed after selecting a location, creating uncertainty about whether the action was processed.

structural_amplification_note: Keyboard navigation reliance amplifies this friction; screen-reader users would similarly lack non-visual confirmation of selection state.

finding: Participant expressed difficulty understanding whether dates were selected and found the selection feedback unclear, requiring manual verification attempts.

finding: Participant experienced difficulty with the date picker because it did not provide clear confirmation that a date had been selected.

structural_amplification_note: Screen reader users are structurally disadvantaged by generic link text because they navigate by link alone without visual context from surrounding content. Keyboard-only users experience the same friction.

finding: Participant expected to see a complete information hierarchy (definition, causes, treatments, management) but the page structure appears incomplete or unclear, causing uncertainty about what content is available.

structural_amplification_note: Screen reader user cohort experiences structural blocking on this interaction; unlabeled or hidden video player controls are fundamentally inaccessible to assistive technology, preventing independent use of the video feature.

finding: Video player controls are not accessible to screen reader users, preventing them from accessing video content and forcing reliance on transcripts as an inferior alternative.

This is a significant component failure that blocks full access to video content for screen reader users, matching S3 and L3.
structural_amplification_note: Blind and screen reader users are structurally blocked from video playback controls. This cohort experiences complete exclusion from a core content pathway that sighted users can access naturally. The availability of transcripts does not mitigate the access barrier to the videos themselves.

finding: Participant could not access video content through the screen reader and missed a substantial part of the page experience as a result.

structural_amplification_note: Screen reader users experience structurally higher friction on video content without proper accessibility markup, captions, or audio descriptions. The participant could not access the full informational and emotional value of the Paralympian videos.

finding: Participant discovered that the accessibility help page does not contain expected accessibility features like font size or contrast controls.
observed_signal: Participant navigated to the accessibility help link, used screen reader navigation to find headings, and began exploring the page content labeled 'Accessibility and additional support' and 'Using our website'.

This is unexpected behaviour relative to user mental model, recoverable but notable.
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader users rely heavily on clear labeling and predictable accessibility feature organisation; a mislabeled or poorly-structured accessibility page creates friction for this cohort by violating established patterns.

finding: Participant using screen reader encountered a table on the comparison page and began to express concern about how it would be presented, cutting off mid-sentence before fully articulating the issue.

stated_signal: So now we have a table, now I personally as a screen reader user.

The incomplete statement and lack of observable struggle prevent a higher severity assignment, but the initiated concern about table accessibility for AT users signals awareness of potential friction. This is moderate friction pending the participant's actual interaction with the table.
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader user; tables without proper markup or structure can significantly impair comprehension for assistive technology users. The participant's incomplete statement suggests anticipated difficulty with table navigation or content extraction.

finding: Participant cannot effectively navigate a 29-row, 3-column data table because screen readers read linearly and the participant lacks Braille access, making it impossible to understand the table structure and content.
observed_signal: Participant attempted to describe how screen readers would read the table (left to right, top to bottom) and acknowledged that without Braille representation, the table structure and meaning would not be accessible.
stated_signal: I'm not a fan of tables because visually I can't see what's in each column, what's in each row. And I haven't really had specific training on how to navigate tables. This table, I don't think is going to make any sense to me at all, because I really struggle reading tables on a computer.
rationale: The participant is a blind or low-vision user relying on screen reader technology. The observed signal (attempting to parse the table structure and recognizing failure) aligns with the stated signal (explicit statement that the table makes no sense without Braille and due to struggle with screen reader table navigation). This is an accessibility friction event where a critical component (the data table) is not usable with assistive technology, blocking access to that specific content or task.
structural_amplification_note: Blind/screen reader users experience structurally amplified friction on tables without proper semantic markup (headers, scopes, summaries) because linear audio rendering destroys the two-dimensional spatial relationships that sighted users perceive visually. The absence of Braille display further reduces access. This cohort requires table structures that expose relationships to AT.

finding: Participant reported complete confusion with table format on the website and stated inability to understand the content structure.

If I see a table format on a website, I will try and ask them to put it into a list because tables just confuse me. They completely confuse me. Without having it physically in front of me in a braille form.
rationale: The participant explicitly stated that the table content is not making sense despite following the structure (reading rows and columns). This is a comprehension friction event: the participant understands the mechanics of tables but cannot extract meaning from this specific table presentation. The stated frustration ('completely confuse me') and the admission of inability to understand what the content relates to align with significant friction. The participant's mention of needing braille form indicates this is a structural accessibility barrier for a screen reader or braille user.
structural_amplification_note: Participant is a braille user or screen reader user (blind/low vision cohort). Table layouts create structural comprehension barriers for this cohort because linearized or screen-reader output of tables without proper semantic markup or simplified alternative formats breaks spatial and relational understanding that sighted users can infer visually. The participant's stated preference for list format confirms that the current table presentation is not accessible for their assistive technology workflow.

finding: Participant using assistive technology could not determine the column context of table cells while tabbing through rows, rendering the table unintelligible despite successful navigation.
observed_signal: Participant tabbed through table rows (row 12, 13, 14, etc.), observed they were progressing through content, but explicitly stated they could not determine what column each cell related to. Participant continued to the end of the 29-row table but could not interpret the data structure.
stated_signal: I'm going down the rows. But I don't know what column this relates to... I can't tell you what this is relating to, cos this is blowing my mind... that whole table to me was in my mind, gibberish, visually, to someone with sight, that would have made sense, but that made no sense to me at all.
rationale: The participant's assistive technology navigation broke the semantic relationship between row headers and column headers. The participant tabbed through all rows but could not extract meaning from the table structure, demonstrating a significant accessibility barrier that was worked around (continuing despite incomprehension) rather than resolved. The stated frustration ('blowing my mind,' 'gibberish') and observed inability to construct meaning from the data align on severity.
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader or keyboard-only navigation does not preserve the visual two-dimensional table structure. Without proper ARIA table markup (scope attributes, thead/tbody, or other semantic relationships), the participant cannot re-establish column context while moving down rows, creating a structural barrier for users of assistive technology that sighted users do not face.

finding: Participant expressed dissatisfaction with the current table format for displaying policy coverage information and suggested a reorganized list-based structure would be better.

stated_signal: It's great because this is under a, this is in a list format, so this list. It's lovely. It's amazing. policies. According I think if you're going to list the two policies, then have underneath each one, a list of bullet points, so what's included and then what's not included because that table.
rationale: The participant's statement shows confusion about the current presentation structure ('that table') and articulates a comprehension or clarity concern about how policies and coverage information are organized. The participant understood the content but found the format confusing and suggested a restructure, indicating medium-level comprehension friction that did not block task completion but created hesitation.

finding: Participant could not understand the table structure or content and stated it was incomprehensible, describing the table as having 29 rows and 3 columns but being unable to extract meaning from it.
observed_signal: Participant encountered a table, attempted to parse its structure and content, then abandoned the table in favor of a summary alternative, stating confusion about what was included in various plans.
stated_signal: I could not tell you what's included, what's not included, what's in what plan. All I know is it's got 29 rows and 3 columns. And then it lost me completely from there. That table blew my mind. It just, unfortunately, for me personally, it didn't make any sense.
rationale: The participant is a blind user encountering a table-based presentation. The stated inability to parse the table structure ('lost me completely') and the observed behaviour of abandoning the table for a summary alternative both indicate significant accessibility friction. The participant worked around the table by using the summary, so the component task was skipped rather than fully blocked, matching S3 and L3.
structural_amplification_note: Blind users relying on screen readers experience structurally amplified friction with complex table layouts. Tables with 29 rows and 3 columns require linearization and nested navigation that screen readers present sequentially, making plan comparison nearly impossible without training or alternative format. The participant explicitly noted that blind people 'really, really, really struggle with tables.'

finding: Participant experienced difficulty understanding a previous table layout and had to compare it to the current layout to grasp the content; the current structure with individual headings for each screening type is significantly clearer for screen reader users.
observed_signal: Participant verbalized that the current table structure is much easier to understand because of the explicit heading structure for each screening type, and explicitly contrasted it with difficulty experienced with the previous table layout.
stated_signal: This is a lot easier to understand because you know what's not included. If the previous table could be set out like this, then it would be a lot easier for screen reader users like myself to understand because it's just a lot more of a straightforward way of putting this all together.
rationale: The participant, a screen reader user, reported that the prior table structure was not accessible and required cognitive effort to parse, while the current heading-based structure resolved that friction. The contrast between the two layouts and the explicit mention of accessibility barriers for screen reader users constitutes significant accessibility friction that was worked around by the improved design. The current satisfaction (E2) reflects relief at the improved structure, not absence of prior friction.
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader users experience structural amplification with non-semantic table layouts lacking proper heading hierarchy. Flat or poorly labeled table structures force users to reconstruct context mentally rather than accessing it programmatically, substantially increasing cognitive load compared to sighted users who can scan visually.

finding: Participant noticed that the Aviva website lacks an accessibility link that was present on the Bupa website, indicating a missing accessibility feature.
observed_signal: Participant navigated the Aviva website using NVDA screen reader and observed the absence of an accessibility link at the top of the page, comparing it unfavorably to the Bupa site.
stated_signal: One thing I've noticed though is the Bupa website had an accessibility link right at the top of the page. The Aviva one doesn't have that so far.
rationale: The participant observed and stated a missing accessibility feature (absence of accessibility link). This is an accessibility friction event, but the participant was able to continue navigation using the skip-to-content link and other available controls without being blocked. The impact is low because alternative accessible mechanisms are present and functioning.
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader users (AT cohort) rely on accessibility links as a standard navigation affordance; their absence reduces discoverability of accessibility resources, though does not block core navigation if other skip links are available.

finding: Participant expressed disappointment that the current website lacks engaging content compared to the Bupa competitor site, which featured sponsor information, service variety, and visual appeal.
observed_signal: Participant compared the current site unfavorably to the Bupa website, noting absence of engaging features and describing the current site as plain with repetitive 'get a quote' messaging.
stated_signal: It doesn't seem as exciting as the Bupa website. The Bupa website was a lot more exciting, like it had a lot more things like that they do, and like talking about the Paralympics that they sponsor and all the different types of things they offer like dental and cancer treatments and, This is all very plain, like, get a quote, get a quote.
rationale: The participant's stated and observed signals align: they explicitly articulated that the website content is unclear in its value proposition and lacks the comprehensibility of engaging messaging seen on the competitor site. The repetitive call-to-action ('get a quote') is perceived as confusing and uninspiring rather than informative. This is a comprehension friction event where the participant understands what they see but finds it lacks meaningful content organization or clarity of purpose.

finding: Participant identified that the accessibility information link is positioned too far down the page, creating a barrier for screen reader users who might miss it.
observed_signal: Participant scrolled to find accessibility information and noted its position on the page.
stated_signal: So the accessibility link needs to be at the top, I personally think. The accessibility bit is right down the page. So that's not really ideal because you might miss it as a screen reader user.
rationale: The participant observed and explicitly stated that the accessibility link placement creates a usability problem for screen reader users. This is an accessibility friction event where the content exists but its location violates good accessibility practice (links should be at the top). The participant completed the task but identified a structural accessibility barrier; this is medium friction without full task blockage.
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader users are structurally amplified by this issue; users relying on sequential link discovery from the top of the page would miss accessibility information positioned far down.

finding: Participant had to scroll significantly to locate information, experiencing reduced discoverability compared to the competing Bupa website.
observed_signal: Participant scrolled through content to find information and made explicit comparison between information visibility on this site versus Bupa.
stated_signal: There's information here, but you have to go quite far down to find it, whereas Bupa, it just, it stands out to me. This is a bit more simple for what you need.
rationale: The participant stated that information requires scrolling significantly to reach and is less discoverable than an alternative product. While the participant did locate the information and the task was completed, the excessive scrolling and effort required to find content created friction. The comparison indicates this is not optimal design; severity is medium because the task was achievable but required more effort than necessary.

finding: Participant expressed dissatisfaction with the placement of the accessibility section, stating it should be at the top of the page rather than halfway down.
observed_signal: Participant explored the Aviva page from top to bottom and identified the accessibility section location as a design issue.
stated_signal: I don't like that the accessibility section is halfway down the page, not at the top.
rationale: The participant completed exploration of the page successfully but expressed uncertainty and mild frustration about the findability and prioritization of the accessibility section. This represents a noticeable but recoverable usability concern; the participant was able to locate the section and complete the exploration without blocking the overall task outcome.
structural_amplification_note: For users who rely on or prioritize accessibility features, the non-prominent placement of the accessibility section halfway down the page amplifies discovery friction, particularly for blind or motor-impaired users who may expect such critical functionality earlier in the page structure.

finding: Participant identified that accessibility features are not prominently positioned on the page and would benefit screen reader users if placed at the top.
observed_signal: Participant is comparing websites using NVDA screen reader and commenting on the layout and accessibility placement.
stated_signal: I think the accessibility part needs to be at the top of the page so it can stand out for screen reader users or other users of assisted technology as well.
rationale: The participant, using assistive technology (NVDA screen reader), observed that accessibility information is not optimally placed for discoverability by AT users. This is accessibility friction—the feature exists but is not positioned to be easily found by screen reader users. The participant recovered and was able to complete the evaluation, indicating medium friction rather than a blocker.
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader user with NVDA; the non-prominent placement of accessibility information structurally amplifies navigation difficulty for AT users who rely on page order and heading hierarchy to locate critical resources.

finding: Participant expressed frustration that the Bupa website content focuses heavily on getting quotes rather than explaining insurance product types clearly.
observed_signal: Participant navigated through the Bupa content and commented on the prevalence of quote-related links and minimal explanatory text.
stated_signal: This is again all about the types of insurance, which is great, but it's all just about like getting a quote. So it doesn't sort of explain all of them very well. It's just links and links to get quotes.
rationale: The participant understood the content structure but found it inadequately explained; the site prioritizes quote acquisition over product clarification. The stated frustration and observed browsing pattern both indicate comprehension friction stemming from insufficient explanatory content. This is noticeable but recoverable friction that does not block task completion.

finding: Participant encountered an image with text overlay that lacked proper alternative text, creating accessibility friction.
observed_signal: Participant noted the presence of text on an image and expressed concern about the quality of this approach.
stated_signal: There's good old text on this image here, which is amazing because I know what the image is of, so that's great.
rationale: The participant's sarcastic tone ('amazing', 'good old') indicates frustration with the text-on-image approach, which is a known accessibility anti-pattern. The participant can see and read it but expressed clear dissatisfaction with this implementation method, indicating accessibility friction that affects usability even if the content is technically accessible in this instance.
structural_amplification_note: This friction is structurally amplified for assistive technology users (screen readers) and those with visual impairments, as text embedded in images is typically not properly conveyed by accessibility tools.

finding: Participant encountered unavailable appointment buttons and expressed uncertainty about their meaning.
observed_signal: Participant clicked on buttons labeled as unavailable and then paused to reason aloud about what the unavailable state indicated.
stated_signal: Button unavailable. Ah, so the unavailable buttons, are where there's no appointments available. I think.
rationale: The participant was uncertain about the meaning of unavailable buttons and had to reason through their purpose. The stated hedging phrase 'I think' and the pause indicate mild confidence friction. However, the participant self-resolved the confusion quickly without blocking task progress, matching S5 and L2.

finding: Participant could not confirm whether the date selection (9th) was actually registered until proceeding to the next step, requiring navigation to a later page to verify the selection.
observed_signal: Participant selected a date, scrolled down, clicked continue, then described checking whether the 9th had been selected by observing the appointments on the next page.
stated_signal: Just to see if it did select the 9th or not. So it has got appointments there. So it had selected the date, but I only knew that through going on to the next steps.
rationale: The participant expressed uncertainty about whether the date selection was confirmed and had to navigate to a subsequent page to verify the action's success. This is wayfinding and confirmation friction typical of F2. The task was ultimately completed, but the lack of immediate feedback on the date selection required an extra verification step, matching the L2 gate (moderate friction; hesitation or non-optimal path; task completed).

finding: Participant pressed enter on a date picker but received no confirmation that the date was selected, and could not determine which date was active.
observed_signal: Participant pressed enter on the date picker and then scrolled, looking for additional information or confirmation of selection.
stated_signal: It just says 9. It doesn't even say that the 9th is selected. This date picker isn't very well thought of here because it should say 9 buttons selected or button 9 selected, so you know you've selected the right date because every button is labeled the same way at the minute.
rationale: Both observed and stated signals indicate the participant was uncertain whether the date selection was confirmed and could not distinguish which button was active. The participant explicitly critiqued the design for not providing clear feedback, and attempted to scroll for additional confirmation before proceeding. This represents significant wayfinding and confidence friction requiring multiple attempts to understand the picker's state.

finding: Participant was unable to locate available appointments displayed by the date picker and noted that no appointment information was visible.
observed_signal: Participant scrolled below the dates looking for appointment information but did not find any visible appointments listed.
stated_signal: So I'm guessing I've got to scroll down below these dates. But it doesn't seem to show you any available appointments though.
rationale: The participant expected to see available appointments after selecting a date but could not locate this information on the page. The stated uncertainty ('I'm guessing I've got to scroll down') combined with the observation that no appointments appeared represents a content-finding friction event where the expected pathway or display was not available or unclear.

finding: Participant identified that the calendar date picker lacks adequate screen reader accessibility because selected dates are not announced to assistive technology users.
observed_signal: Participant described the calendar layout as not being super accessible for screen reader users and noted that the selected date is not communicated.
stated_signal: the calendar, maybe if it could be like a combo box that has the day, the month, the year. That would be a bit easier because the way this calendar is laid out, it's not super accessible for screen reader users because you don't know what date you have selected.
rationale: The participant, a screen reader user, explicitly stated that the calendar component fails to announce the selected date, making it inaccessible for AT users. This is a significant accessibility barrier that creates confusion about the current state. While the overall booking process was praised, this component failure represents a barrier that AT-dependent users must work around or skip.
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader users experience structurally elevated friction on this calendar control because selected state and date values are not properly exposed to assistive technology, preventing independent confirmation of selections.

finding: Participant identified that a visual element (new symptom indicator) needs to be made more prominent or bold to be more obvious to users.
observed_signal: Participant reviewed the page content, noted the various sections (expert, interaction types, causes, symptoms, etc.), and specifically called out the new symptom element as needing improvement in visibility.
stated_signal: I would make this more bold or more obvious, um, just so that...
rationale: The participant encountered a design element that did not stand out as expected given its importance. The stated concern about visibility aligns with the observed behaviour of noticing the element but judging it insufficiently prominent. This is a minor design friction with low impact on task completion.

finding: Participant encountered an unexpected ad element on the page and expressed confusion about its purpose and how to dismiss it.
observed_signal: Participant paused navigation, questioned the presence of the ad, noted it was there, then asked how to remove it without completing that action.
stated_signal: Oh, is this, why is this here? Um, oh, OK, um. Oh, OK, um, oh, OK, this is just access, there's an, there's that's an ad, OK, um. Self-help Um OK, that can go away. How do you get rid of?
rationale: The participant observed an ad element that disrupted their navigation flow, questioned why it was present, and attempted to remove it. Both stated confusion and observed hesitation indicate unexpected interface behaviour that momentarily interrupted task flow but was recoverable; the participant did not abandon the task.

finding: Participant was unable to access or view video content and expressed confusion about how to enable it, attributing the issue to cookie settings.
observed_signal: Participant clicked on a video, questioned whether it was a video, attempted to access a transcript instead, and ultimately could not proceed with video viewing.
stated_signal: I struggle to hear people's voices in videos. I don't know how to change my cookies in order to watch it. I think that might be what's causing the issue.
rationale: The participant encountered uncertainty about how to enable video playback and expressed confusion about the cause (cookies) but recovered by using the transcript alternative. The stated confusion and observable workaround behaviour both indicate wayfinding and confidence friction; the participant was able to continue via an alternative pathway, placing this at S5 (noticeable but recoverable).
structural_amplification_note: Participants who rely on transcripts or have hearing difficulties may experience amplified friction when video accessibility settings are unclear or non-obvious.

finding: Participant accidentally changed a setting to 'reject' and did not know how to reverse the change.
observed_signal: Participant stated they accidentally changed something to reject and expressed uncertainty about how to undo it.
stated_signal: I don't know how to change it accidentally to reject, so I don't know how to change it.
rationale: The participant experienced an unexpected or accidental state change and lacked clarity on how to reverse it. The stated confusion and recovery via transcript use indicate medium confidence friction; the participant did not appear to be fully blocked but expressed genuine uncertainty about the interface behaviour.

finding: Participant expected video playback to trigger cookie settings but instead had to navigate to a separate location, causing confusion about the interaction model.
observed_signal: Participant pressed play on a video and then realized they needed to click elsewhere and navigate to another location to access cookie settings.
stated_signal: I wish that if I pressed play it would pop up with the cookie settings, I didn't realize I had to click on there and go to another thing to watch, like actually click on it, so that was a bit um yeah. That was a bit annoying.
rationale: The participant's stated expectation (play button triggers cookie settings popup) conflicted with the actual design (separate navigation required). The participant expressed annoyance and experienced a non-optimal path, but recovered and completed the action. This is a single instance of unexpected behaviour with brief frustration and quick recovery, fitting S5 and L2.

finding: Participant could not locate the accessibility information and searched through multiple menu sections before eventually finding it under contact us.
observed_signal: Participant looked at the bottom of the page, checked 'help and support', then 'contact us', and finally found 'Accessibility and additional support' under contact us.
stated_signal: It's not there. Help, it's under help and support. I actually cannot find, OK, I can't find the accessibility. Oh, yeah, so it's under contact us.
rationale: The participant explicitly stated inability to find the accessibility information and searched through multiple incorrect menu locations before recovering. The stated frustration ('I actually cannot find') and observed multi-step search align, indicating brief wayfinding friction that was resolved without task abandonment, matching the S5/L2 gate.

finding: Participant had difficulty locating the accessibility information page and expected it to be under 'Help and Support' rather than 'Contact Us', describing the location as 'very not in a great spot' and 'not very obvious'.
observed_signal: Participant navigated to the page and found it under 'Contact Us' > 'Accessibility and Additional Support', but expressed that this was an unexpected location.
stated_signal: I found the page, that was very not in a great spot. Um, I thought it'd be under help and support, uh, not contact us. This was very hard to find. It wasn't very obvious.
rationale: The participant successfully located the accessibility information but experienced wayfinding friction due to unexpected information architecture. Both stated frustration ('very hard to find', 'not very obvious') and observed navigation behaviour (had to search in an unexpected location) align on moderate friction. The task was completed despite the poor discoverability, matching the S5/L2 gate (brief hesitation, one correction, recoverable friction).
structural_amplification_note: For users relying on assistive technology or magnification, difficulty finding accessibility-specific support pages represents a structural barrier that may amplify friction for the intended accessibility-dependent cohort.

finding: Participant expressed that the accessibility reporting feature should be located under 'Help and Support' rather than 'Contact Us', indicating confusion about information architecture and wayfinding.
observed_signal: Participant reviewed the accessibility features and commented on the placement of the report function.
stated_signal: I personally think it should be under help and support, not contact us. That's where I automatically go if I need accessibility help or something. I can't find it; it should, everything could be under there.
rationale: The participant stated explicit uncertainty about where to find accessibility help and indicated that the current information architecture (Contact Us) does not match their mental model (Help and Support). The participant expressed this as an improvement suggestion rather than a blocked task, indicating medium friction with recoverable wayfinding confusion.
structural_amplification_note: For users relying on accessibility features, unclear placement of accessibility-related functionality creates additional cognitive load when seeking help.

finding: Participant found the color scheme inaccessible in dark mode and had to disable dark mode to read the page content properly.
observed_signal: Participant stated the color combination was problematic, then disabled dark mode to improve readability.
stated_signal: My dark mode does not like the color schemes, this is the worst combo of colors, um, we've, mustard yellow, as I say, or light blue... I'm just gonna turn this off so I can actually read this properly.
rationale: The participant explicitly identified a contrast or color accessibility problem in dark mode that made content unreadable. They required a workaround (disabling dark mode) to proceed. This is accessibility friction with multiple failed attempts at normal use before recovery, matching S4 and L3.
structural_amplification_note: Dark mode users experience structurally higher friction when color contrast and color scheme combinations are not tested across accessibility modes.

finding: Participant felt overwhelmed by the amount and density of information and imagery on the first page.
observed_signal: Participant navigated to the page and immediately expressed that there is a lot of information, short information fragments, and many images.
stated_signal: There's a lot of information on the first page, there's a lot of short information and a lot of images, but not... I can already tell with the color it's very overwhelming.
rationale: The participant stated the page felt overwhelming due to excessive information density and imagery. While they did not abandon the task, the observation indicates noticeable cognitive effort required to process the layout. This is medium friction with a hesitation but no full impediment to proceeding.

finding: Participant found the page layout overwhelming with too many images and insufficient information clarity, describing it as poorly assembled.
observed_signal: Participant navigated through the page, clicked to explore content areas (life insurance, home insurance, health), then expressed concern about the page design and structure.
stated_signal: This is very overwhelming, um. Look, I love the images, um, but it's too much, it's too much and not a lot of efficient information, um, to me this looks like a website that was just chucked together. Um, this looks like um. Like a news agency with all these images and just these headings to try and grab your attention, without explaining anything.
rationale: The participant's navigation was successful, but the stated feedback reveals comprehension friction related to unclear information hierarchy and excessive visual noise without sufficient explanatory content. The participant recovered and continued navigating, indicating noticeable but not blocking friction. The stated frustration and observed successful progression align on a medium friction level.

finding: Participant encountered unexpected behaviour when attempting to interact with a clickable element that did not behave as anticipated.
observed_signal: Participant said 'if I click on this, I assume, yeah, so it opens up the, oh, it does not like, yeah' indicating an interaction did not produce the expected result, then moved to close it.
stated_signal: if I click on this, I assume, yeah, so it opens up the, oh, it does not like, yeah, um, so I'm just gonna close that
rationale: The participant's action ('oh, it does not like') suggests an interface element responded unexpectedly to a click. The participant recovered immediately by closing and moving on without impediment to the overall task. This is a minor, isolated incident with negligible impact on task progress.

finding: Participant discovered that August dates are not clickable on the date picker, creating uncertainty about the interaction pattern and available date range.
observed_signal: Participant attempted to click August dates, found they were not selectable, and verbally questioned whether they were missing something or doing something wrong.
stated_signal: I can't click any dates on here, uh, am I missing? So I can click for April. I can click for May. I can cook for June and July. But I can't do August, OK.
rationale: The participant expected all month buttons to be clickable based on the pattern of April through July, but August did not respond as expected. The stated uncertainty ('am I missing?') and observed attempt indicate F5 (unexpected behaviour). The participant recovered quickly by selecting July instead, matching S5 and L2 (brief hesitation, one correction, mild confusion, non-optimal path with quick recovery).

finding: Participant expressed dissatisfaction with pricing on the current platform and stated intention to switch to a competitor product.
observed_signal: Participant mentioned Claude is asking for more money and stated they need to switch to Gemini instead, indicating they are considering abandoning the current product.
stated_signal: Claude is asking me to pay more money. So now I need to go to Gemini.
rationale: The participant's stated desire to switch to a competitor due to cost indicates fundamental dissatisfaction with the product's value proposition. This is a blocker to continued product use, as the participant is actively seeking an alternative. The combination of stated intention to leave and negative sentiment (E5) reflects high-friction friction that prevents project outcome achievement.

finding: Participant demonstrated skepticism toward search results and stated a preference to skip sponsored content in favour of non-sponsored results.
observed_signal: Participant verbalized distrust of initial search results, indicating they do not naturally follow the default search ranking or sponsored placements.
stated_signal: I don't typically go for the first like search results, cause I know they're sponsored. I typically like to go further than sponsored results.
rationale: The participant's stated caution about sponsored results reflects confidence friction—uncertainty about whether to follow the default search order. This is a minor wayfinding concern that requires extra cognitive effort but does not block the task. The participant has a clear recovery strategy (scroll past sponsored results), so this is medium-to-low friction.

finding: Participant reported feeling overwhelmed by long menu bars and expressed this as a deterrent to using the product.
observed_signal: Participant stated unprompted discomfort with menu length and described it as a reason to avoid engagement.
stated_signal: typically things like when your menu bars are too long, I get very overwhelmed very quickly. And so it would sort of put me off.
rationale: The participant identified long menu bars as a source of cognitive overload that would discourage their use of the product. This is stated friction related to excessive cognitive effort in navigation; severity is S5 because the concern is stated but did not block task completion in this window, and calibrator is L2 (hesitation and expressed reluctance affecting willingness to engage).

finding: Participant actively searched for and had to locate the search and login functions, indicating they were not immediately discoverable.
observed_signal: Participant stated they were looking for search and log in, then navigated to find them, suggesting a discovery step was required.
stated_signal: I was looking for search and log in, so this.
rationale: The participant had to search for key entry points (search and login) rather than finding them immediately, indicating wayfinding uncertainty. This is a brief, recovered search with neutral narration; it represents one friction moment of medium intensity without blocking task completion.

finding: Participant unable to click the reject cookies button and felt forced to accept cookies, expressing strong dissatisfaction with the interaction.
observed_signal: Participant attempted to click the reject cookies button but could not activate it; stated the button was not clickable and then proceeded to accept cookies instead.
stated_signal: It's not letting me reject cookies, which is not nice. That's gonna force me to accept them, which I absolutely don't like. I don't know why it's not letting me click that, but I can't click that. Something like this would actually put me off.
rationale: The participant explicitly stated the reject button was not clickable and expressed frustration and concern about forced acceptance. The observed inability to activate the button combined with stated negative emotion and complaint about credibility damage aligns with high friction—the participant was blocked from their preferred action and forced into an undesired workaround, significantly affecting trust.

finding: Participant discovered that the displayed symptoms did not match the symptoms of the person they were looking up information for.
observed_signal: Participant reviewed the symptom list presented (disturbed sleep, smaller handwriting, losing sense of smell, fatigue, needing to pee, anxiety, depression) and explicitly stated most of those symptoms are not what the person they know is confronted with.
stated_signal: It's interesting because most of those symptoms are not what the person I know is confronted with.
rationale: The participant found that the interface returned symptoms that did not align with their expectation or the actual symptom presentation of the person they were researching. This is unexpected behaviour relative to the participant's mental model of what symptoms should appear for the ailment. The participant noted this observation matter-of-factly and continued, indicating recovery without task abandonment, fitting S5 and L2.

finding: Participant relied on search to locate content and would not have found it through the alphabetical categorization alone, indicating the categorization logic was unclear or unintuitive.
observed_signal: Participant used the search function at the top of the page to find the content and stated that without search, they would not have clicked on the alphabetical category where the content was located.
stated_signal: I didn't know that's how you'd categorize it. So if I hadn't done search, I probably wouldn't have even clicked there.
rationale: The participant experienced wayfinding uncertainty about the categorization logic and was uncertain whether the content would be located under the category where it actually appeared. However, they successfully reached the correct page using search as a workaround and did not abandon the task, indicating recoverable friction with a non-optimal path.

finding: Participant discovered that Parkinson's disease was not found under the dementia category where they initially expected it, and was surprised and concerned by its absence from an age-related health conditions section.
observed_signal: Participant navigated to health information by topic, looked for Parkinson's under dementia, then checked for it under aging/mature health conditions, and expressed surprise that it did not appear there.
stated_signal: I don't know that Parkinson's is classified as dementia. So actually going here, I wouldn't have been able to find it. I would have probably then had to go and say, Sure. It would actually be hard because the symptoms are so different. The fact that it doesn't show up here is quite interesting. For me, Parkinson's is an age related illness, and the fact that it doesn't show up here is quite interesting.
rationale: The participant could not locate Parkinson's disease in the expected categorical pathways (dementia, then aging/mature health) and expressed surprise and concern about its absence. The stated and observed signals both indicate search difficulty and unmet expectations. This is noticeable friction with emotional valence (surprise, mild frustration) but the participant recovered by considering alternative pathways and did not abandon the task.

finding: Participant would have struggled to find information about dementia without the search function, indicating the primary navigation pathway (A to Z listing) was inadequate for discovering this health topic.
observed_signal: Participant stated the search function was their first option, A to Z would have been second, and other classification methods would not have helped at all.
stated_signal: Having not known that it's actually classified as a dementia, I would have probably struggled if you guys didn't have the search function. My first option would have definitely been search. My second option would have been A to Z. That other classification wouldn't have helped me at all.
rationale: The participant indicates that without search, they would have struggled to locate dementia information, and the A to Z browsing structure was insufficient as a primary discovery method. The stated difficulty combined with reliance on search as a workaround represents a significant content findability barrier that was only overcome through the search feature, fitting S3 (significant component failure worked around).

finding: Participant identified missing medication information on the health information page and expressed uncertainty about whether their parents' medications are appropriate.
observed_signal: Participant described a gap in the content (lack of medication type information) and articulated confusion about medication appropriateness, saying 'I don't know if these are even the right things' when referring to boxes of pills.
stated_signal: Something else that would have probably been helpful is like medications... Is it treated with non-steroidal anti-inflammatories? Is it treated with ibuprofen?... I don't know if these are even the right things.
rationale: The participant cannot locate medication-related information needed to verify treatment appropriateness for their parents' illness. While the participant articulates the need clearly and completes their thought process, the stated frustration ('I don't know if these are even the right things') indicates noticeable friction. This is a content gap rather than a complete task blocker, fitting S5 and L2.

finding: Participant perceived the initial page view as information overload and expressed concern about excessive content density.
observed_signal: Participant scanned the page and immediately characterized it as having 'a lot of information' and described the layout as information overload from first view.
stated_signal: It's got a lot of information. That's my first thought. Um, I tend to find that when a website, when a screen is filled only by the footer, then I consider that website to actually have quite a lot of information overload in the beginning.
rationale: The participant identified excessive information density and complex page layout as a barrier to efficient comprehension from the outset. The stated concern about overload and observed first-impression negative reaction align on this friction event. This represents noticeable friction affecting initial engagement but does not block task progression; the participant is articulating the concern and continuing to evaluate the content.

finding: Participant expressed discomfort with dropdown menus that overlay other content, perceiving them as cluttered and problematic for information architecture.
observed_signal: Participant provided critical feedback on menu design approach during review.
stated_signal: I'm not sure how I feel about menus that pop up over other content. I, I tend to find that quite cluttered.
rationale: The participant identified an unexpected or undesired interface pattern (overlaying menus) that conflicts with their mental model of clean information presentation. The stated concern aligns with observed critical reflection. This is a noticeable design friction that affects perception but does not block task completion.

finding: Participant noted that the 'Get a quote' call-to-action was not prominently positioned at the top of the page where similar high-priority prompts are located.
observed_signal: Participant reviewed page layout and identified suboptimal placement of a primary action.
stated_signal: Get a quote would have been really nice if it was very easily accessible somewhere at the top here, because it's a, it's an action, it's a prompt and calls to action are really nice when they are very easy to see.
rationale: The participant expressed uncertainty about action availability and wayfinding expectations for a key call-to-action. The stated concern and observed review behaviour both point to a design discoverability issue that reduces confidence in finding this action, but does not prevent eventual completion.

finding: Participant expressed confusion about font inconsistency across the interface, uncertain whether different visual treatments indicate functional differences or are merely stylistic variations.
