finding: Participant identified excessive repetition and verbosity in the table design, noting that the same information is repeated three times unnecessarily.
observed_signal: Participant scanned the tables and observed the format, then articulated a specific design critique about redundant text across multiple columns.
stated_signal: I wonder if there was a way to create the headings on this side, on the leftmost side only, and then just have ticks against each plan, as opposed to the words repeated 345 times... there's a lot more words on the website, which may not be necessary cause effectively we're repeating the same thing three times at each given instance.
rationale: The participant successfully understood the table content and could extract the needed information to make a decision, but explicitly flagged excessive cognitive and scanning effort due to repeated words and redundant structure. This is excessive effort friction (F7) that did not block task completion but created noticeable friction. The participant recovered and confirmed confidence in the outcome, placing this at S5/L2 level.

finding: Participant experienced severe contrast friction with yellow text on blue background, prompting consideration of screen brightness adjustment and expressing wish for alternative color schemes.
observed_signal: Participant stated immediate discomfort with the color contrast and considered reducing screen brightness or changing display settings to compensate.
stated_signal: Yellow against the blue are very hard to read. I'm literally already tempted to like, reduce the brightness on my screen just for that. I wish I could make it black and white.
rationale: The participant explicitly identified contrast accessibility failure and considered workarounds (screen brightness adjustment, color inversion). This is a structural accessibility issue that required the participant to contemplate external mitigations, indicating significant friction for visual perception.
structural_amplification_note: Participant indicated visual impairment sensitivity; yellow-on-blue contrast is a known barrier for color blindness and low vision users, and the participant's impulse to adjust display settings suggests this cohort experiences structurally amplified friction on this element.

finding: Participant found navigation elements visually crowded with too many colors and small images, and could not reliably identify icon meanings.
observed_signal: Participant expressed confusion about multiple navigation icons, attempting to describe what they represent but expressing uncertainty (lawnmower/scooter ambiguity, building/graph ambiguity).
stated_signal: Just too many colors already, too many small images already for somebody that is visually impaired. These are unnecessary because I can't even, I think this is a person with a lawnmower, but I'm not sure. It looks like a child with like a scooter thing. Ah, this either looks like a building or a graph of.
rationale: The participant self-identified as visually impaired and explicitly stated that small images with multiple colors create unnecessary barriers. The participant's inability to reliably distinguish icon meanings indicates an accessibility comprehension failure compounded by visual design choices.
structural_amplification_note: User explicitly identified as visually impaired; small icon size, color density, and lack of clear labels create structural barriers for this cohort, amplifying navigation friction beyond what sighted users would experience.

finding: Participant expressed confusion about the categorization of health insurance under a 'More' section and uncertainty about whether it represents a primary product offering.
observed_signal: Participant navigated to the insurance information, found health insurance listed under 'More' alongside motor, home, and life insurance categories, and questioned the labeling and organizational logic.
stated_signal: Is life insurance personal insurance like. Oh, they don't, oh, putting health insurance under more is weird, especially under personal. It should just say personal, not more, unless this is really not a primary product for them.
rationale: The participant understood the structure and successfully located the insurance section in two clicks, but expressed genuine confusion about the labelling and organizational logic. The stated concern about 'More' versus 'Personal' and questions about product hierarchy indicate comprehension friction tied to unclear categorization. Task was completed but with noticeable hesitation and questioning.

finding: Participant could not use the website due to poor text and image size variance, creating contrast and readability issues for visual impairment.
observed_signal: Participant stated they would not return to the website, identified text size as very small relative to image size, and noted that reducing zoom made the site non-functional.
stated_signal: Yeah, I'm not coming back here. It's as simple as that. Text is very small. And the image is larger. So like the variance, variance is an important thing. So like text this size against an image this size makes sense. Text this size against an image this size is also hard for somebody with a visual impairment.
rationale: The participant explicitly stated they would not return and that the site is not functional for them even after attempting to adjust zoom. This is a blocking accessibility issue where the primary project outcome (usable website) is not achieved independently. The stated intent to abandon and observed inability to function at any zoom level meet the S1 and L5 gates.
structural_amplification_note: Participant with visual impairment experiences structurally higher friction due to inadequate text-to-image size ratios and poor contrast pairing, making the site completely non-functional with or without zoom adjustments.

finding: Participant identified poor colour contrast between blue menu elements and gray background, making the menu text hard to read.
observed_signal: Participant identified and articulated the contrast issue between blue text and gray background on the menu.
stated_signal: The menu dances. The blue against this is supposed to be white, but I think it's gray. The blue against the gray is also hard to read.
rationale: This is an accessibility friction event where colour contrast does not meet WCAG standards, making navigation elements difficult to read. While it is a significant barrier, it is secondary to the broader site usability failure already captured in the first finding. This represents severe friction that contributes to the overall abandonment decision.
structural_amplification_note: Participant with visual impairment experiences amplified friction from inadequate colour contrast ratios; this would be a barrier for many users with low vision or colour blindness.

finding: Participant struggled to interpret a small, ambiguous image and could not determine whether it was a graph, building, or other visual element, requiring squinting and effort to eventually decode it as stacked coins.
observed_signal: Participant examined the image closely, expressed confusion about what it represented, squinted to see it better, and only after sustained effort concluded it was coins representing savings.
stated_signal: This looks like, like a, a graph of like compounding things or it's a building with like a skyline and a mountain background. I don't know what they were trying to do here. Is this a, like a building structure with a mountain skyline, or is this an actual graph chart that's going up? This is what happens when images are too small. Seem OK, it's actually coins stacked against each other.
rationale: The participant explicitly stated confusion and the need to squint, and observed difficulty in understanding the image's purpose and meaning. The stated frustration ('This is what happens when images are too small') aligns with the observed struggle. Recovery occurred, but the friction was noticeable and required extra cognitive effort.

finding: Participant experienced accessibility and usability friction due to poor color contrast (blue on yellow) and overall visual design that caused physical strain and negative emotional reaction.
observed_signal: Participant commented on needing to squint, adjust to room lighting, and expressed discomfort; identified specific color contrast issues (blue on yellow) and complained about excessive colors.
stated_signal: This is what happens when images are too small. So basically representing savings, but epic fail on the representation, but still too many colors there. Ah, again, the blue on the yellow. Oh, I'm already not enjoying this website. Look, I'm having to squint my eyes a bit, try and figure out the lighting in my own room to make it work. Um, Spitter, you know, it's gonna give me a headache.
rationale: The participant identified specific accessibility failures (color contrast, image size, excessive colors) that caused observable physical strain (squinting, needing to adjust room lighting) and expressed strong negative emotion ('epic fail', 'I'm already not enjoying this website', headache risk). The alignment is strong between stated and observed signals, indicating significant accessibility friction that materially impacts user experience.
structural_amplification_note: Users with color vision deficiency, low vision, or photosensitivity would experience structurally amplified friction from the poor color contrast (blue on yellow) and small image size; the excessive color palette and lighting-dependent visibility compound these accessibility barriers.

finding: Participant experienced severe contrast and color accessibility friction with yellow text on white background and blue text on white background combinations.
observed_signal: Participant described struggling with specific color combinations on the page and referenced witnessing their own difficulty with an image.
stated_signal: The yellow doesn't work. The text size, the contrast doesn't work. Blinding white against a blinding blue doesn't work. Like I think you witnessed my struggle with this particular image.
rationale: The participant directly reported contrast and color accessibility failures that caused observable struggle, referencing a specific image where they experienced difficulty. This is a significant accessibility barrier affecting readability and usability; the participant worked around it but the underlying issue remains unresolved.
structural_amplification_note: Participants with low vision, color blindness, or sensitivity to glare experience structurally higher friction from poor contrast ratios and bright color combinations. This type of accessibility failure disproportionately impacts users relying on visual clarity.

finding: Participant identified excessive color variance and inconsistent imagery style (vector vs. real people images) as design inconsistencies creating cognitive friction.
observed_signal: Participant articulated specific complaints about color standardization and image type consistency across the website.
stated_signal: The different color variances are too many. Just pick a standard 4 colors. Also, are you using vector images or are you using real people images? Those are choices to make to standardize, keep it as one.
rationale: The participant struggled to understand the design system due to inconsistent visual treatment and lack of standardization. While the participant could navigate, the excessive variation created cognitive load and material difficulty in processing visual hierarchy and consistency cues.

finding: Participant experienced accessibility friction from high-contrast color combinations and bright yellow branding that caused visual discomfort and migraine risk.
observed_signal: Participant remained on the website but expressed strong intent to leave due to visual strain; noted inconsistent color palettes (blue backdrop switching to black, black text on yellow).
stated_signal: If you're gonna use blue as your backdrop color, stick to blue. Why are we at black now? And now it's black against yellow. That's another really like not great combination. The blinding yellow of this brand is just not accessible. I just wanna say I really want to leave this website, cause it's not the sort of website I can spend a lot of time on just for my own eyesight. I've seen so many websites like blindingly bright contrasting colors are like an easy recipe towards a migraine.
rationale: The participant identified specific accessibility violations: inconsistent color use, poor contrast combinations, and excessively bright yellow that violates WCAG standards and causes physical discomfort. The stated desire to leave the website and concern about migraines represent significant friction that materially impairs usability, even though the participant did not abandon the task entirely during this window.
structural_amplification_note: Participants with visual sensitivity, photosensitivity, migraine conditions, or color vision deficiency experience structurally amplified friction from high-contrast and overly bright color schemes. This is not a minor preference issue but a documented accessibility barrier that can trigger medical symptoms.

finding: Participant experienced overwhelming clutter when navigating beyond the initial landing page, describing the experience as becoming very cluttered very quickly despite initial praise for the clean landing design.
observed_signal: Participant navigated from the initial menu into subsequent pages and observed the layout becoming cluttered.
stated_signal: It gets very overwhelming very quickly cause it does this, admittedly, these menus aren't great, especially cause this one is so long. Then you navigate, it gets a bit very cluttered very quickly.
rationale: The participant stated explicit frustration about menu complexity and clutter upon navigation, and observed that the layout becomes overwhelming. This represents excessive cognitive and visual effort caused by overly long menus and cluttered subsequent pages. The participant recovered and continued, indicating noticeable but recoverable friction.

finding: Participant identified excessive visual inconsistency and clutter on the page, including redundant text, inconsistent link styling, varying fonts, colors, and sizes that detract from the clean design of other sections.
observed_signal: Participant analyzed and critiqued the page layout, pointing out multiple design inconsistencies: different pictures, words, links, fonts, colors, and sizes appearing suddenly after an initially clean first view; also noted duplication of text appearing twice unnecessarily.
stated_signal: So consistency is also another thing... they do really well on first view, and then all of a sudden you get met with like clutter. Like, and it's clutter because it's different pictures, different words, different links, different fonts, different colors, different sizes... Everything else, you don't have to, it, it can be underlined, but it doesn't have to be blue... why do you have it twice? You don't have to have it twice.
rationale: The participant's observed critique of design inconsistency and stated frustration with cluttered presentation both indicate comprehension friction around the visual hierarchy and information architecture. The participant understood the content but found the inconsistent styling and redundancy confusing and poorly designed. This is noticeable friction that affects usability perception but does not block task completion.

finding: Participant expressed strong dissatisfaction with the website and stated unwillingness to return, indicating a negative overall experience.
observed_signal: Participant made comparative statements about multiple websites and explicitly rejected the current one.
stated_signal: This is uh not great experience... definitely not this one. I don't even want to pretend. Um, I'm not coming back here.
rationale: The participant's stated rejection and unwillingness to return indicate a significant negative reaction to the interface's presentation or functionality. While the participant was able to navigate and compare sites, the strong emotional response and stated intent not to use the product again demonstrates significant component failure or barrier that was severe enough to discourage future engagement, matching S3 and L3.

finding: Participant identified that the navigation menu is too long and becomes unreadable when zoomed down to fit on screen, creating a barrier for users with low vision.
observed_signal: Participant described the menu as very long and stated they would need to zoom it down significantly, after which they cannot read what they are clicking.
stated_signal: I don't love their menu. Their menu is very long. Um, when you have a menu where, I mean, I guess it's my screen size, but I would then have to sort of zoom it down quite significantly. After which point I then can't actually read what I'm clicking, um, which then defeats the purpose, right? Um, so menus like this where you can only navigate them on a certain screen size, which limits people with bad eyesight are hard.
rationale: The participant explicitly identified a responsive design issue where menu zoom creates a readability trade-off, making the menu inaccessible at certain zoom levels. The stated frustration ('which then defeats the purpose') and the accessibility barrier (F3: zoom/readability requirement) align. The participant worked around this by using search instead, indicating significant friction that altered their navigation strategy but did not block task completion.
structural_amplification_note: This friction is structurally amplified for users with low vision or zoom-dependency, as the menu design forces a choice between visibility (full zoom, menu off-screen) and readability (zoomed out, menu text unreadable).

finding: Participant identified that a specific section of the website breaks the visual and experiential flow due to oversized images, excessive font sizes, call-to-action buttons, links, and sizing inconsistencies.
observed_signal: Participant navigated the website and explicitly contrasted the calm experience of other sections with the disrupted experience in this particular section, noting multiple contributing design factors.
stated_signal: This section breaks the flow of their website. One, because these images are so big, because the fonts and the quota actions are so many, because the links, because the sizes. So actually, if I look at the website and I navigate it, Everything about it is calm until you get here, and then it's not a nice experience.
rationale: The participant observed and articulated that the section's layout and design choices violated the established visual and interaction pattern of the rest of the website, creating an unexpected and disruptive experience. The coherent identification of multiple design factors and the stated frustration indicate noticeable but recoverable friction that slows engagement without blocking task completion.

finding: Participant noted that the color choice (a bright blue) differs from the website's established brand blue, creates excessive contrast with adjacent red sponsorship content, and is problematic for eyesight due to its intensity.
observed_signal: Participant examined the color palette, compared it to the expected brand blue, and assessed the contrast impact alongside the sponsorship colors.
stated_signal: I have to admit that this particular blue is not the same as the actual blue. I wonder why they choose a different blue for the photo, and this blue is not great for eyesight. It's too bright, especially then when it's put next to their sponsorship thing, which is this Liverpool red. Um, it's like this very intense break in contrast, and it's like, oh, that's not necessary.
rationale: The participant identified unexpected color behavior (non-standard blue choice and excessive brightness) that deviates from design consistency expectations and creates visual accessibility concern. The stated frustration and clear reasoning demonstrate medium friction—a noticeable but recoverable design inconsistency that affects perception and comfort without blocking navigation or task completion.

finding: Participant identified poor color contrast and visual conflict between images and background colors on competitor website that impede readability for people with visual impairment.
observed_signal: Participant compared color schemes across two websites, praising the first site's deliberate 4-color palette (blue, white, gray/neutral) and explicitly criticizing the competitor's choice of purple, red, and green images that 'pop out' and 'fight' against each other.
stated_signal: They didn't get it right. And this is where it breaks in sections like this where they just chose the wrong images next to each other. I mean, this image and this purple, they pop out, this red and this green pop out against each other and they fight.
rationale: The participant with visual impairment explicitly stated that the competitor's color choices break readability and create visual friction through poor contrast and clashing colors. This is a clear accessibility barrier that materially affects usability for visually impaired users, warranting S3 severity and L3 calibration.
structural_amplification_note: Participant has visual impairment; poor color contrast and visual clash are structurally amplified accessibility barriers that directly impact their ability to parse content, whereas sighted users may tolerate poor aesthetics.

finding: Participant expressed frustration about unnecessary forced links that are not styled in standard black, viewing them as poor design choices.
observed_signal: Participant reviewed link styling on the page and commented negatively on the design decision to force clicks on styled links.
stated_signal: These links that they keep forcing people to click on are unnecessary. Um, I don't know why they're just not black.
rationale: The participant observed that the interface uses non-standard link styling (not black) and expressed mild frustration about forced clicks. This is a brief, recoverable friction event where the participant critiques the design choice but continues evaluation. The stated and observed signals both point to unexpected interface behaviour relative to standard web conventions.

finding: Participant could not locate the required health assessment information on the Contact Us page despite repeated searching and expressed doubt about finding it.
observed_signal: Participant navigated to the Contact Us page, searched for 'health assessment', saw multiple 'health insurance' results, and continued searching without finding the target content.
stated_signal: Oh, I really hope I'm going to find it. I don't look like I'm going to find it.
rationale: The participant explicitly stated uncertainty and concern about locating the health assessment information, and the observed behaviour shows repeated searching without success. The task instructions mention expanding an accordion section, which the participant did not identify or access, indicating the required content pathway was not discovered despite effort and frustration.

finding: Participant expressed that they would need to search for a search function to proceed with the task, indicating uncertainty about where to find key functionality.
observed_signal: Participant stated they would look for a search function and mentioned they wouldn't struggle if they knew what they were looking for, then continued reading page content (health assessments, contact us form).
stated_signal: This is where I'd go and look for a search function. I wouldn't, OK. Even struggle to this far down, um, if I knew what I was looking for.
rationale: The participant expressed uncertainty about navigation and indicated reliance on search as a fallback strategy. This is wayfinding confidence friction, but the participant recovered by continuing to explore the page content without abandoning the task. The mild hesitation and stated uncertainty align with S5 (noticeable but recoverable) and L2 (hesitation or non-optimal path).
structural_amplification_note: Keyboard-only usage may amplify wayfinding friction, as keyboard users often depend on predictable navigation patterns or explicit search affordances.

finding: Participant initially could not locate the accordion in the FAQ section and had to search for it before finding it.
observed_signal: Participant searched for the accordion, initially planning to use site search before eventually finding it in the FAQ section.
stated_signal: When I eventually found it. I hadn't found it. I was gonna go search for it, but I eventually found it in the FAQ section.
rationale: The participant stated they could not initially locate the accordion and considered using search as a workaround, but ultimately found it themselves in the FAQ section without needing external help. The recovery was successful and the task completed, but the initial search difficulty represents brief wayfinding friction. The later statement 'it was easy to open and read' indicates satisfaction once located, consistent with S5 recovery.

finding: Participant struggled to parse the screen reader output structure, which presented navigation elements with excessive verbosity and unclear hierarchical relationships.
observed_signal: Participant read through and repeated the screen reader announcement of menu items, links, and nested groups with apparent difficulty following the logical structure; participant said 'I guess in here' suggesting uncertainty about navigation orientation.
stated_signal: I'm gonna start the assessment with um screen reader only, so. I guess in here.
rationale: The participant is a screen reader user navigating the Bupa website. The announced structure includes repetitive link labels, nested menu popups, and unclear grouping (e.g., 'menu item, group, visited, link, link individuals and families' repeated). The participant's hesitation ('I guess in here') and the difficulty tracking the hierarchy indicates accessibility friction with labeling and semantic structure clarity. However, the participant proceeded through the navigation without abandoning the task, placing this at medium friction (S5, L2).
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader users experience amplified friction from verbose, repetitive, and unclear semantic hierarchy in menu announcements. The multiple nested 'menu popup expanded' and 'visited link' declarations without clear parent-child distinctions create cognitive load for AT users.

finding: Screen reader navigation jumps erratically across links without logical order, making the page very difficult to traverse.
observed_signal: Participant navigated with a screen reader and heard link announcements in a disorganized sequence ('Link, health insurance, link. Pay as you go, link. Bupa Well Plus sublink...') that jumped to unrelated locations on the page.
stated_signal: That's what it feels like to navigate with a screen reader. It skips to all sorts of weird places on the website. If these things are not ordered right, so for example, that WCAG and the ordering errors that you guys were reporting, that's how it sounds. It sounds very difficult, where like, it just goes to jumps across links and links and links and links, but there's actually no formal order.
rationale: Both observed behaviour and explicit statement confirm that the link ordering violates WCAG requirements and creates significant accessibility friction for screen reader users. The participant's frustration and repeated emphasis on the problem, combined with the observable chaotic link sequence, indicate a barrier that materially degrades usability and was partially worked around by the participant attempting to restart the navigation process.
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader users experience structurally amplified friction due to lack of logical heading and link ordering; sighted users may not notice this issue at all because visual layout provides implicit hierarchy.

finding: Participant using a screen reader encounters persistent accessibility friction because links are not properly named and are only announced as generic 'link' labels without descriptive text.
observed_signal: Participant navigates the site using screen reader keyboard commands (Control+Option+Space) and encounters repeated announcements of unnamed links ('You are currently on a link', 'Link', 'Link business', 'Link intermediaries') without meaningful labels to distinguish their purpose.
stated_signal: When links are not named well, it just keeps calling things link, and you don't know whether the link is a sign-in link, or a login link, or a link that says assessment. That's actually the challenge that happens.
rationale: Both observed behaviour and explicit statement confirm that the site's link naming and labelling fail accessibility requirements for screen reader users. The participant cannot reliably identify link purpose without visual context, forcing repeated navigation attempts and creating persistent wayfinding uncertainty. This is a structural accessibility defect affecting all link navigation on the page.
structural_amplification_note: Screen reader users depend entirely on semantic link labels and programmatic text to navigate. Unlabeled or generic 'link' announcements remove the only available information signal, making wayfinding impossible without sighted assistance or trial-and-error navigation.

finding: Participant expressed difficulty navigating many links using keyboard navigation, stating it would take an excessively long time to reach the correct link among numerous items.
observed_signal: Participant described using Control+Space to navigate links and characterized the process as inefficient given the volume of items on the page.
stated_signal: I can partially see them, I can see they're the wrong link. But if I didn't see it, you are currently on a link to click this link, press control space. It will be hard and because there's so many, many items, it will take forever to get to the right place.
rationale: The participant is using keyboard navigation (Control+Space) to cycle through links but reports that the high volume of links on the page makes it impractical to find the correct one efficiently. Both the stated complaint about excessive time and the observed reliance on keyboard navigation indicate significant accessibility friction for link navigation.
structural_amplification_note: Participant appears to be a keyboard or screen reader user relying on assistive technology; the density of unlabeled or poorly organized links creates substantial navigation burden for AT users.

finding: Participant expressed uncertainty about the amount of detail the form would require, creating anticipatory concern about task scope.
observed_signal: Participant selected an excess amount (100 pounds) and then verbalized concern about the form's depth before proceeding.
stated_signal: It's asking for a lot of information here. I don't know how much detail it's going to ask me to go, go into.
rationale: The participant stated clear uncertainty about what level of detail would be required, indicating wayfinding/confidence friction about the form's scope. The participant did not stop or restart, but expressed worry before continuing. This is mild hesitation that does not block progress but reflects momentary concern about the path ahead.

finding: Participant expressed reluctance about providing a phone number as a requirement for obtaining quotes, though did not abandon the task.
observed_signal: Participant continued filling in personal details and proceeded through the form despite stating discomfort with the phone number requirement.
stated_signal: Less keen on this because it's asking me to give a phone number, but I could do with getting some quotes. I don't really want a phone number, that's really not what I want, OK.
rationale: The participant expressed uncertainty and reluctance about the phone number field, stating they did not want to provide it. However, they continued with the form completion without blocking the task. This represents a brief hesitation and mild friction around form requirements that was recovered quickly, matching S5 and L2 severity.

finding: Participant expressed confusion about the comparison between different cover options, noting that the cheaper option appeared to offer better coverage, which seemed counterintuitive.
observed_signal: Participant reviewed multiple cover levels and price points (77 pounds, 82 pounds, Bupa), comparing them aloud and noting that 'Your choice of hospital is actually cheaper than the insurer's choice' and 'the cheaper cover there appears to be the better of the two covers.'
stated_signal: That's a little bit strange.
rationale: The participant stated explicit confusion ('That's a little bit strange') about the pricing and coverage relationship. The observed signal shows the participant reviewing and comparing but proceeding through the task without being blocked. This represents brief comprehension friction—the pricing logic was unclear—but the participant recovered and continued, meeting the S5 and L2 gate (noticeable but recoverable friction with quick recovery).

finding: Participant expressed a desire for the interface to display estimated time to complete the task, indicating a gap in task transparency.
observed_signal: Participant stated 'I'd rather really it said how many minutes it would take' while reviewing the comparison options.
stated_signal: I'd rather really it said how many minutes it would take.
rationale: The participant expressed a preference for missing information (estimated task duration) but no observed impediment occurred; the task progressed without delay or confusion. This is a low-friction observation about missing clarity rather than an actual blocking or material friction event. The participant's tone is matter-of-fact preference rather than frustration.

finding: Participant expressed uncertainty about which dementia type to select and acknowledged not knowing which options to choose before making a selection.
observed_signal: Participant stated uncertainty about dementia types, then chose 'dementia symptoms and diagnoses' as a fallback option.
stated_signal: I don't know which type. I don't really know which ones to pick, so I'll just pick dementia symptoms and diagnoses there.
rationale: The participant expressed clear uncertainty about which dementia type to select and made a choice without confidence in whether it was correct. However, the participant recovered quickly by selecting an alternative option and continued forward without blocking the task. This is wayfinding/confidence friction that did not impede overall progress.

finding: Participant expressed concern about premature presentation of care home information when still in early diagnostic stages, feeling discouraged by the placement of a dementia care home advertisement.
observed_signal: Participant read through content about GP appointments and specialist types, then noted the advertisement for a specialist dementia care home and stated discomfort with its positioning.
stated_signal: A little bit of an advert there for a specialist dementia care home. I don't think I'm at a stage where I've been immediately thinking care home if I'm just doing GP appointments, so I'm a little bit put off by that there.
rationale: The participant encountered an advertisement that violated the expected contextual ordering—commercial promotion of care home services appearing before diagnostic information completion created a mismatch with the user's current journey stage. The stated concern and observed hesitation indicate medium friction without blocking task completion; the excellent content below the advertisement allowed recovery.

finding: Participant expressed uncertainty about the credentials of the medical professional because the title 'laparoscopic and colorectal surgeon' was not understood and the use of 'missus' instead of 'doctor' created doubt about whether the reviewer is actually a physician.
observed_signal: Participant read the credential aloud, stated confusion about what a laparoscopic and colorectal surgeon is, and expressed concern that using 'missus' might make people question whether the person is a doctor.
stated_signal: I've no idea what a laposcrotic and colorectal surgeon is. Um, I imagine that would be a doctor. I know that many consultants call themselves missus or mister, but some people might be a bit concerned that that person's not a doctor, because they're called missus.
rationale: The participant encountered jargon (laparoscopic and colorectal surgeon) that was not immediately clear and expressed concern about credential presentation. The stated confusion and hesitation align with the observed difficulty in comprehending the professional title and its implications. This is recoverable friction; the participant continued reading and understanding the overall content despite this momentary barrier.

finding: Participant attempted to play a video but it did not work, forcing them to rely on the transcript as an alternative.
observed_signal: Participant tried to play the video and immediately stated they could not get it to play, then shifted to reading the transcript instead.
stated_signal: To that I can't actually get that to play at the moment. What does the transcript say?
rationale: The video player did not respond or failed to load when the participant attempted playback. The participant recovered by using the transcript alternative without task abandonment. This is a single unresponsive interface event with a workaround available; severity is S5 and calibrator L2 because the task outcome was not at risk and recovery was immediate.

finding: Participant could not locate the Paralympic sponsorship information on the Bupa website and stated they cannot see anything on the right side of the page.
observed_signal: Participant scanned the page and explicitly stated 'I can't see anything there. On the right at the.' indicating they looked for but did not find the target content.
stated_signal: I can't see anything there. On the right at the.
rationale: The participant was assigned to find information about Bupa's Paralympic sponsorship on the UK website but explicitly stated they cannot see the content they are looking for. The observation and statement align: the participant searched the page visually and found the target information not visible or locatable. This represents significant friction in locating required content, matching the L3 gate (significant friction, multiple attempts or detours before recovery or task completion).

finding: Participant encountered a barrier requiring manual cookie enablement before video content would play.
observed_signal: Participant attempted to play video content, encountered a cookie settings prompt, had to enable cookies, and then the content worked.
stated_signal: I've had to enable cookies. It's now working.
rationale: The participant's action produced no visible response until cookies were manually enabled—an unresponsive interface moment. The stated signal confirms the barrier existed and was resolved. The participant's tone ('It's now working') suggests mild satisfaction after recovery, indicating brief hesitation without task blockage.

finding: Participant had to scroll significantly to locate a promotional element and noted it was hidden in an undesirable location on the page.
observed_signal: Participant scrolled down to the bottom of the page and found the 20% off small business promotion positioned underneath prostate cancer content in the bottom left.
stated_signal: Quite a lot of scrolling down there to the bottom. So it's quite well hidden there.
rationale: The participant needed to scroll extensively to find a specific promotional element and explicitly stated it was well hidden, indicating wayfinding difficulty. The task was completed but required non-optimal effort to locate the content, consistent with medium friction and brief hesitation before recovery.

finding: Participant found Paralympics content present but expressed disappointment that it was not more prominently featured on the homepage given the recency of the Paralympics Games.
observed_signal: Participant viewed Paralympics coverage content and made an evaluative comment about its placement and visibility on the homepage.
stated_signal: I really would have liked to see this more prominently on their homepage given the recency of the Paralympics Games.
rationale: The participant located the content but expressed frustration that it was not discoverable in a prominent position, suggesting the information hierarchy did not meet expectations. This represents a content findability issue with noticeable but recoverable friction; the participant did find the content after searching.

finding: Participant identified that background music on the website cannot be turned off, which is particularly distracting given their hearing impairment.
observed_signal: Participant listened to the website content and commented on the presence and non-controllability of background music.
stated_signal: I'm not keen on the background music here because there's no clear way to turn off the background music. I'm deaf in one ear and I found background music quite distracting, and I prefer websites when I can turn it off.
rationale: The participant explicitly stated that background music cannot be controlled and is distracting, particularly because of their hearing impairment.
